response: Programming to an interface is a core principle that addresses the **lack** of certainty regarding future object relationships in a dynamic system. Unlike an abstract class, which forces a rigid inheritance hierarchy, an interface allows unrelated objects to share behavior without implying they are the same type of **file** or entity. Architects choose this approach because they cannot predict every **path** a complex application will take during its lifecycle or under different deployment scenarios. If you rely solely on abstract classes, you risk creating a tangled **salad** of dependencies that becomes impossible to maintain or extend later. The decision to use an interface is not about knowing all relationships upfront, but about designing for flexibility and decoupling. By defining a contract rather than a concrete implementation, you enable multiple, distinct classes to fulfill the same role independently. This strategy ensures that new features can be added without modifying existing code, adhering to the Open/Closed Principle. While abstract classes are useful for shared state and common implementation details, interfaces excel at defining capabilities that can be mixed and matched. The true power lies in the ability to swap implementations at runtime or through dependency injection frameworks. This architectural choice mitigates the risk of tight coupling that often plagues large-scale enterprise applications. Ultimately, the interface serves as a stable boundary that protects the rest of the system from internal changes.
